behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is primarily a module-handoff risk (file discovery -> ordering -> execution), not just a pure sort helper. REAL-INFRA/LLM/MULTI-STEP checks: behavior text does not require real OS/network/subprocess contracts, no AI output dependence, and no agentic chain. Rejection-gate: System rejected because the behavior text only specifies ordering logic by filename prefix and does not require a real external contract check beyond local module wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test migrations directory with out-of-order filenames, run the runner against a test DB, and assert execution order by reading applied versions from `_migrations` in sequence.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure appears in interaction between migration history persistence and runner filtering across runs. REAL-INFRA/LLM/MULTI-STEP: no explicit real subprocess/network requirement, no AI dependence, no agentic sequence. Rejection-gate: System rejected because behavior text requires idempotency across runs, which can be validated with local DB readback in an integration test without external protocol contract assertions.
behaviors.test_description: Run the runner twice on the same test DB; verify the migration SQL side effect occurs once and `_migrations` contains one record per version (no duplicates/new entries on second run).

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is transaction + control-flow interaction across runner, DB transaction wrapper, and migration loop. Integration-brake: (a) no explicit OS/network/subprocess contract required in text; (b) no AI output dependence; (c) no multi-agentic chain; (d) no LLM-stage handoff. Rejection-gate: System rejected because the behavior text is about transactional rollback and stop-on-error within the runner/database interaction, not an external API/process contract.
behaviors.test_description: Use migrations where one statement intentionally fails between successful migrations; run once and assert failed migration effects are absent (rolled back), prior committed migrations remain valid per design, and later migrations were not attempted/recorded.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: behavior explicitly requires concurrent execution from separate processes; this is a subprocess/coordination reality check that unit/integration fakes can miss. SYSTEM TRIAD trigger: externally visible side effect must be proven by round-trip DB readback showing exactly-once application across both instances. Rejection-gate: Integration rejected by explicit text 'two instances ... at the same time' and 'across both instances', which requires real process-level concurrency behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Launch two real runner processes concurrently against the same Postgres instance with overlapping migration set; wait for completion; verify final schema/data and `_migrations` show each version recorded once and no duplicate side effects.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the `_migrations` table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this checks initialization sequencing between metadata bootstrap and migration execution across modules. REAL-INFRA/LLM/MULTI-STEP: no required real OS/network/subprocess contract in text, no AI dependence, no agentic chaining. Rejection-gate: System rejected because behavior text specifies startup ordering on a fresh DB, which can be validated in integration with local DB state inspection.
behaviors.test_description: Start with an empty test DB and at least one migration file; run runner and assert `_migrations` exists before first migration record/action (e.g., via instrumentation or observable ordering), then verify normal application proceeds.
